question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the legislation his Department intends to bring through in the remainder of the 2022-2027 Assembly mandate.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail any regard that is given to the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 when considering applications for temporary variations to school enrolment.
answer_text: The Temporary Variation policy recognises Integrated education, irrespective of management type, as a distinct school sector. As a result, additional places at Integrated schools are normally approved, unless a suitable place is available for the children concerned at another Integrated school. In doing so, my Department meets its statutory duties under the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether any assessment has been undertaken of the impact of approving a temporary variation on the sustainability of neighbouring schools.
answer_text: Temporary Variation (TV) policy is child-centred therefore decisions are based on the needs of the child(ren) concerned as opposed to the needs of institutions.  As part of the decision-making process, when considering a TV request, my Department consults with the Education Authority which has overall operational responsibility for planning of school provision, and with the Council for Catholic Maintained Schools, when appropriate, as the planning authority for Catholic Maintained schools.  Their views are considered and help inform any decisions, as they are responsible for maintaining a sustainable network of schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the criteria used to assess applications for temporary variations to school enrolment; and (ii) how the potential impact on neighbouring schools’ enrolment and viability is weighted within that process.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the current (i) status; and (ii) timeline of the A4 Enniskillen Southern Bypass.
To ask the Minister of Health whether he has made any bid to the Minister of Finance for NHS dentists.

To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will consider the reinstatement of midwifery, dentistry and chiropody to Rathmore Health Centre, Belleek.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has any plans to provide free period products for sporting clubs.
answer_text: Under the Period Products (Free Provision) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, the Department is responsible for ensuring access to free period products through specified public service bodies. Sporting clubs are not included within the scope of bodies designated under the legislation or the associated Regulations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has any plans to introduce teaching apprenticeships.
answer_text: Initial Teacher Education (ITE) in Northern Ireland is provided by the four local Higher Education Institutions (HEIs): Stranmillis University College, St Mary’s University College, Queen’s University Belfast and Ulster University, through the established qualification routes of a fouryear Bachelor of Education degree or a oneyear Postgraduate Certificate in Education.My Department has no plans to introduce teaching apprenticeships to Northern Ireland however through the TransformED Strategy work is underway, in partnership with the local HEIs, to introduce a part-time ITE pathway which will widen access and provide greater flexibility for those wanting to undertake ITE.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the multi-year budget process.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any assessment that has been made of the process for the Proposed Draft Budget 2026-29/30.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the timescale on when works will be carried out on the mini roundabout in Ederney, County Fermanagh.
answer_text: The Department plans to renew the road markings at the mini roundabout in Ederney within the next 8 weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rationale as to why the Bowel Cancer Screening service has been removed from the South West Acute Hospital, Enniskillen.
answer_text: A small number of bowel screening endoscopy clinics were provided at the South West Acute Hospital (SWAH), during 2022, as a temporary measure to address backlogs as a result of the COVID-19 pandemic.  The last clinic provided in the SWAH was 8 November 2022.The Western Trust is currently providing clinics at Omagh Hospital and primary care complex for the population within the Fermanagh and Omagh District Council and at Altnagelvin Hospital for the population within Derry and Strabane District Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any funding available for secondary schools to build sports halls.
answer_text: On 2 December 2024, I made a statement launching the Curriculum-Led Capital Investment Programme. The purpose of this programme is to target investment in priority areas that will support and enhance delivery of the Northern Ireland Curriculum.  One of my objectives under this programme is to provide all post-primary schools with access to a sports hall, to support the delivery of PE within the curriculum.  My Department undertook a survey of schools in November 2024, to identify the level of existing provision. The schools which responded stating they did not have access to a sports hall and met basic eligibility criteria were selected for inclusion under this strand of the Curriculum-Led Capital Investment Programme These schools were advised by letter on 2 February 2026.Previously, a number of schools were approved to advance in design for the provision of a sports hall under the School Enhancement Programme (SEP). These projects are currently being progressed and there are no current plans for a further call under SEP.I will continue to make the case for additional capital funding for Education through the Executive.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any steps being taken to review the needs of women and girls facing additional risk of Violence Against Women and Girls, as outlined in the First Delivery Plan 2024-2026.
answer_text: Violence against women and girls affects everyone in our society, but we know that some women and girls face greater risks to their safety and additional barriers to accessing justice and support. This is why we are committed to ensuring that future action is shaped by lived experience. Last October, along with the DAERA Minister, we launched the Community Led Review on Rural Women and Girls, led by the Rural Community Network and the Northern Ireland Rural Women’s Network. This work will help us understand the lived realities of rural women and girls and inform practical, rural sensitive recommendations. Community-led reviews focusing on deaf and disabled women and girls and migrant women and girls are in their early stages. Initial engagement with key stakeholders has taken place to help shape the focus of this work.The Regional Change Fund is supporting a number of activities that are working with those groups that face additional risk. This includes translating some programme materials into different languages and providing a translator at workshops.Violence against women and girls is unacceptable, and we will continue to do everything we can to stop it.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will undertake a feasibility study for a pedestrian crossing on Queen Elizabeth Road, Enniskillen, from the Cornagrade Road side to the Jobs and Benefits Office side of the road.
answer_text: As an assessment of this location has not previously been undertaken, I have asked my officials to complete a vehicle and pedestrian survey to determine if the location meets the threshold for the provision of a controlled pedestrian crossing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the Loughshore Road, Belleek, which is subsiding, will be repaired.
answer_text: I can advise that the site at Loughshore Road, Belleek is subject to ongoing monitoring due to inherently weak ground conditions. At present, it is not possible to provide a definitive timeline for full repairs, as persistent saturation from recent heavy rainfall continues to affect the area. Officials are actively assessing the situation, and it is anticipated that repair strategies and a projected reopening date will be determined by 16 March 2026. In the interim, verge embankment repairs are scheduled for completion before the end of February 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a timescale of when white line painting will be carried out in Belleek, County Fermanagh.
answer_text: Road markings serve a very important function in conveying information and requirements about the road network and poorly maintained road markings can have a negative impact on road safety. With this in mind, my officials undertake regular routine maintenance inspections of all roads during which all defects, including defective signs and road markings, are noted. Road markings are not replaced within a routine/specific time frame, rather when approximately 30-40% of the marked area is found to be ineffective or worn away.  Where the condition of road markings falls below the required standards, they are renewed as soon as possible within the available funding limits.  Repairs are prioritised to concentrate on regulatory road markings, such as stop lines, and those longitudinal lines necessary for continued road safety.The Department awarded two new road marking contracts for the Western Division last summer; the appointed contractor is currently working through a schedule of outstanding works.  This schedule has been prioritised to complete those locations with a road safety need first concentrating on the trunk road network. Given the backlog of work and the current unsuitable weather conditions, it will likely be late springtime before the lining renewal works in Belleek are complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any support that is being provided to landowners, community groups, and the local council in Fermanagh to manage infected ash dieback trees safely.
answer_text: My Department provides guidance on the safe management of ash dieback on its website. Landowners can access Forest Service’s Tree Safety Management Practice Guide, which outlines riskbased procedures for inspecting and managing affected trees.Most woodlands, despite the impact of ash dieback, can continue to develop and provide the range of benefits associated with a woodland land use. On that basis my Department continues to make annual premia payments to landowners over the course of their individual forestry grant schemes.Where woodlands have been impacted to the extent that felling is required, my Department has provided support to landowners through the Woodland Investment Grant (Restocking). This scheme encourages woodland owners to create more resilience to disease through species choice and diversity. It is expected that the scheme will reopen for applications later this year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any plans in place to replace lost tree cover due to ash dieback, to protect biodiversity and public safety in Fermanagh.
answer_text: Ash dieback is now established in the wider environment in Fermanagh and across Northern Ireland. Ash dieback is no longer a quarantine pest and, on that basis, affected trees are not required to be removed as a disease control measure.Most woodlands, despite the impact of ash dieback, can continue to develop and provide the range of benefits and biodiversity associated with a woodland use.Where woodlands have been impacted to the extent that felling is required, my Department has provided support to landowners through the Woodland Investment Grant (Restocking). This scheme encourages woodland owners to create more resilience to disease through species choice and diversity. It is expected that the scheme will reopen for applications later this year.It is important for landowners and managers to exercise their public safety responsibilities regarding the management of risk associated with affected trees and woodlands. Forest Service conducts regular treesafety inspections across its estate. Removed trees are replaced through natural regeneration or replanting, following approved forest design and biodiversity plans. Landowners can avail of guidance published by my Department’s Forest Service including a Tree Safety Management Practice Guide which sets out its procedures for managing affected trees through a risk-based inspection programme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any measures his Department is taking to address the spread of ash dieback in Fermanagh.
answer_text: Due to the confirmation of the extensive presence of ash dieback in the wider environment it is no-longer classified as a quarantine pest in plant health legislation in the EU and the UK, and therefore no plant health official controls apply. In this context, monitoring and surveying of ash trees for any change over time is a matter for individual landowners at a local level consistent with their duty of care and public safety responsibilities.Land owners can avail of guidance published by my Department’s Forest Service including a Tree Safety Management Practice Guide (https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/tree-safety-management-practice-guide), which sets out its procedures for managing affected trees through a risk based inspection programme. The risk zones are established taking account of such things as the tree’s proximity to public facilities, residences, workplaces, forest and public road access.  The Forest Service has, in the past, made training available on tree safety management to partner organisations, which staff from Fermanagh and Omagh District Council attended.Woodlands affected by ash dieback can be naturally regenerated with a wide diversity of tree species, which improves resilience to tree disease. My Department is involved with ongoing research into strains of the disease. Scientific research aimed at identifying any tolerances within the ash tree population and tree breeding programmes have been commissioned in partnership with DAFM and DEFRA.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the crash barrier that was removed for resurfacing on the A46, Loughshore Road, County Fermanagh, will be replaced.
answer_text: My officials advise that the recently resurfaced length of A46 Loughshore Road did not have a vehicle restraint system in place prior to the work.  The completed works included the resurfacing of the carriageway and the provision of a supporting verge for part of the scheme. A section of the new verge is currently subject to settlement and further works will be carried out in the during the summer period to raise the verge level.  Upon completion of this work, an assessment will be undertaken to determine the need and priority of a permanent vehicle restraint system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to address staff concerns about the accuracy and reliability of encompass data, given reports that many staff have reverted to maintaining manual records.
answer_text: Data quality within encompass is monitored by the Trusts, and corrective action is taken where issues are identified. Trusts have not provided any evidence to substantiate the assertion that “many staff” are reverting to paper. System usage data—which records all activity across roles and departments—would indicate if there were widespread non-use of the system, and no such pattern has been identified. As unauthorised parallel or informal records can create inconsistencies and pose potential patient safety risks, Trusts and the regional encompass team continue to ensure there are structured feedback routes and clear mechanisms for staff to raise concerns or request further support to reduce the likelihood of these occurring. As with any clinical system, the quality of outputs depends on the quality of information entered. Trusts will continue to support staff to use encompass safely, consistently and effectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the full quality assurance of encompass data, as referenced on his Department's website, has been completed.
answer_text: The quality assurance of encompass data for official statistics purposes is ongoing and remains a high priority, particularly for data relating to HSC Trusts that have most recently transitioned to the system. All statistics sourced from encompass continue to be classified as Official Statistics in Development which are a subset of Official Statistics in line with the Code of Practice for Statistics. Work is actively progressing to ensure that the data meets the required quality standards, however data relating to Social Care and Mental Health services has not yet reached a stage of maturity or stability to support publication.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department will take to ensure that validated, accurate baseline information on children and adults with autism and ADHD is available before any new initiatives or commissioning decisions are made based on recent reviews.

To ask the Minister of Health whether he will provide a timeline for the resumption of quarterly autism and ADHD statistics, including referrals for assessment, to ensure transparency and informed decision-making.
To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on Ireland's Hidden Heartlands.
answer_text: In December, a licensing agreement was agreed between Tourism Northern Ireland and Fáilte Ireland, which means that eligible County Fermanagh tourism businesses can now use the Ireland’s Hidden Heartlands brand. To support this, I allocated an additional £300,000 to Tourism NI to promote this opportunity.Tourism Northern Ireland have developed a County Fermanagh specific marketing campaign to promote the announcement, which will launch on 16th February across Ireland. The campaign is expected to deliver almost 27 million advertising impacts and reach 58% of the population and will be supported by industry offers. An industry engagement event was held on 11th December in the Manor House Hotel. To date, Tourism NI have received 35 submissions from the Fermanagh industry to use the Ireland’s Hidden Heartlands brand logo, including Lough Erne Water Taxi and Finn Lough.Fáilte Ireland will air a TV commercial in the Republic of Ireland market – in the week commencing 19th January – promoting County Fermanagh as part of the Ireland’s Hidden Heartlands Visitor Proposition – and Tourism Ireland will continue promoting the brand, working with local partners on overseas campaigns. The Ireland's Hidden Heartlands brand will also feature in the Tourism NI Spring Marketing campaign from 9 February.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the housing scheme planned for the Cornagrade Road, Enniskillen.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“A proposal for up to 60 new social homes (over two phases) on the Cornagrade Road, Enniskillen was previously included in the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) for delivery by Woven Housing Association. However, during the 2025 SHDP bidding round (new social housing proposals submitted to the Housing Executive by Housing Associations for the period 2026/27 – 2028/29), Woven confirmed that the scheme proposals for the Cornagrade Road could no longer be progressed due to two key factors: (1) that a Ground Investigation survey demonstrated that the extent of piling was greater than originally anticipated; and (2) NI Water confirmed that there was no capacity within their infrastructure to accommodate the proposed scheme.The association advised that they are actively seeking other new social housing development opportunities in Enniskillen, which are at an early stage of feasibility.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) why people living in supported living are excluded from Discretionary Support; and (ii) whether his Department has any plans to review this policy, given the lack of alternative support during the transition to Universal Credit.
answer_text: There is no automatic exclusion from Discretionary Support for people in supported living. A range of support is available under Discretionary Support, including the Universal Credit New Claims Grant.  Full details can be found on the NI Direct, Finance Support  which can be accessed via the following website link www.nidirect.gov.uk/campaigns/finance-support

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail why individuals moving from Employment and Support Allowance to Universal Credit are required to make an new claim rather than being automatically migrated, resulting in a five week wait for first payment.
answer_text: Anyone moving to Universal Credit (UC) since migration commenced in October 2023 has been required to make a fresh claim. This is necessary to ensure all aspects of entitlement are captured as Universal Credit replaces a range of legacy benefits and Tax Credits. All those migrating from Employment Support Allowance receive a run on payment of their legacy benefit two weeks after having made a claim to Universal Credit. In addition, my Department’s Universal Credit New Claims Grant, which is unique to Northern Ireland, is available to assist those awaiting their first payment, subject to meeting the eligibility criteria.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any liaison there is between his Department, supported-living providers, and the Health and Social Care Trusts to manage cases where a claimant’s inability to pay charges during the Universal Credit waiting period may put their placement at risk.
answer_text: AnswerTenants living in supported living accommodation will continue to have their rent charges met through Housing Benefit.  My Department has frequent engagement with NIHE (who administer Housing Benefit), Housing Associations and Social Care Trusts to ensure that Universal Credit claimants, including those in supported living accommodation, are provided with the necessary support throughout their Universal Credit claim journey.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any immediate actions being taken to address the extreme pressure at Altnagelvin Area Hospital, including the reported use of non-bed spaces for patients awaiting surgery.
answer_text: I recognise the significant pressures currently facing Altnagelvin Area Hospital and the impact this has on patients and staff. Among the five largest Type 1 Emergency Departments (EDs) in Northern Ireland, Altnagelvin Hospital has recorded the lowest number of 12-hour breaches and a comparatively high proportion of patients assessed and treated within four hours in 2025/26, compared to other EDs.However, Altnagelvin Hospital continues to face significant challenges.  My Department has taken actions to support immediate improvements in patient flow at the hospital, working closely with the Western Trust to address pressures.An enhanced Minor Injuries Unit opened in March 2024 and the Ambulatory Care Unit has been expanded to support patient flow and reduce admissions where clinically appropriate. The consultant-led respiratory hub will be expanded ahead of this winter to run seven days per week.A control room and discharge co-ordination team has been established to optimise patient flow and facilitate timely discharges. The assistant director for unscheduled care and medicine now attends daily on-site co-ordination meetings to strengthen oversight and expedite decision-making.A revised hospital escalation plan, integrated with the regional escalation dashboard, is being embedded to ensure system-wide consistency and transparency.Workforce capacity has been enhanced, with additional acute medical staff rostered at weekends and the night service manager team strengthened to improve out-of-hours oversight. Recruitment is also underway for three additional emergency medicine consultants.Patients awaiting elective surgery may, on occasion, be asked to wait in a designated area until a bed becomes available. To create further capacity, escalation spaces, including undesignated bed areas, are being utilised within medicine and surgery. These spaces are subject to rigorous risk assessment and only patients deemed clinically appropriate are placed in them.My Department remains committed to patient safety and sustaining services under significant pressure and is working in partnership with the Trust to monitor and manage these challenges effectively.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline what the Executive requires from the upcoming Autumn Budget 2025.
To ask the Minister of Finance when the Baby Loss Certificate Scheme will become operational.
answer_text: The introduction of Baby Loss Certificate Scheme has taken an important step forward following the Assembly’s approval of the Further Consideration Stage on 18 November.  The Final Stage is scheduled for Tuesday, 2 December.I want to assure you that work is progressing at pace to draft the Baby Loss Certificate Scheme Regulations. These will set out the details of how the scheme will	 operate ensuring it provides the recognition that bereaved parents deserve. I am hopeful that the Bill will come into force before the end of December 2025. This will allow me to bring the Regulations to the Finance Committee and Assembly early next year and outline a clear timetable for the scheme’s introduction. Should the Regulations proceed through the Assembly scrutiny and approval process without delay, I expect to the scheme to be operational by the end of this financial year. I will set out a timetable for their introduction once the Bill has completed its passage through the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has any plans to (i) review; or (ii) amend, existing legislation governing the naming of streets.
answer_text: The Traffic Signs Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1997 legislation, for which my Department has responsibility, does not govern the naming of streets. The naming of streets is governed by the Local Government (Miscellaneous Provisions) (Northern Ireland) Order 1995.  Article 11 of this Order grants Councils the power to name streets and number properties.  As the legislation relating to the naming of streets is not within my portfolio, I do not have any plans to review or amend.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of consultant surgeons that are employed in the Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT), broken down by (i) their specialisms, (ii) whether they are part-time, locum or permanent; and (ii) whether their contracts are for all locations in the WHSCT.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail a schedule for the installation of gas to Enniskillen, and surrounding areas, with particular reference to housing developments and estates.
answer_text: As Minister for the Department for Communities, I have overall responsibility for the Residential Buildings sector chapter within the draft Northern Ireland Climate Action Plan.  However, while the chapter contains policies and proposals to reduce emissions within the Residential Buildings sector, many of these fall to other Departments to deliver.  The provision of a schedule for the installation of gas to Enniskillen and surrounding areas, would be for the Minister for the Economy to answer, as this policy area falls within her remit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount that the Blueprint Programme has cost from 2006 to date.
answer_text: The Pathology Blueprint Programme was established by my Department in 2022 as part of a strategic initiative to transform and modernise Health and Social Care pathology services.  The Programme aims to establish a single management structure for all pathology and blood transfusion services in the form of a new regional pathology services agency by April 2027.  It forms a key part of the diagnostic capacity element of my Three Year Plan to stabilise, reform and deliver health and social care, which I published last year.The table below sets out the costs for each full year of the Programme to date:Financial YearProgramme Costs £K2021/222202022/232202023/243002024/25220

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he would consider a review into the Sure Start programme, with a view to expanding it to be a universal service.
answer_text: My department recently commissioned an independent review of Sure Start which reported that the programme is appropriately targeted by supporting children in the most socially disadvantaged areas of Northern Ireland, as these children are most at risk of educational underachievement.In 2024-25, as a preliminary action within the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy (ELCS), I expanded Sure Start to an additional 22 socially disadvantaged areas. In 2025-26 I am expanding the programme further through a new Outreach model to support children and families with evidenced need for support, regardless of geographical location. Any further expansion of Sure Start will be considered within implementation of the ELCS, subject to availability of Executive funding and capacity within the Early Years sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department's vision plan for the South West Acute Hospital.
answer_text: The Western Trust Board has now taken the decision to close the current Public Consultation on the reconfiguration of Emergency General Surgery in the Western Trust and will now move forward on visioning work for ‘Future Health and Care Services in Fermanagh and West Tyrone’.I understand that the Trust plans to work with all relevant stakeholders to develop a clear vision for health and social care provision in Fermanagh and West Tyrone, including the role of and services provided in SWAH. This detailed analysis will be informed by population needs. Careful consideration will also be given to key strands of policy and research which will help shape the strategic and policy framework going forward.I share the Trust’s commitment to the long-term sustainability and development of SWAH as a vital and successful hospital within the HSC regional network of hospitals.I would expect any vision plan developed by a Trust to align with the objectives and commitments of my Three-Year Plan, published in December 2024, and the Reset Plan, published in July this year.My officials are working closely with Western Trust colleagues on the scope of the work to be undertaken.It is my firm view that SWAH remains an important Acute Hospital within the NI hospital network and I look forward to seeing a clear vision for the population in Fermanagh and West Tyrone.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on his engagement with the British Government on the Local Growth Fund.
To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Skills Action Plan.
answer_text: I launched the Skills Action Plan on Thursday 16th October at the NI Chamber of Commerce Future Workforce Summit. In line with my Economic Vision, I have worked closely with my officials to develop a forward-looking framework that will equip our people with the skills required to meet both the current demands and future ambitions of our economy.A central element of our approach has been to identify opportunities for collaboration with industry and education partners, fostering innovative ways of working. We are committed to strengthening our skills system by building on the proven success of our institutions, interventions and programmes. Through continued investment in our universities, further education colleges and apprenticeships, we will ensure that individuals are equipped with the knowledge and capabilities needed to thrive in an increasingly dynamic and competitive labour market.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he expects to see the vision plan on the South West Acute Hospital from the Western Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Western Health & Social Care Trust has now taken the decision to close the current Public Consultation on Emergency General Surgery and plan to work with all relevant stakeholders to develop a clear vision for health and social care provision in Fermanagh and West Tyrone, including SWAH.The Western Trust are committed to the long-term sustainability and development of SWAH as a vital and successful hospital within the HSC regional network of hospitals. My officials are engaging with Trust colleagues to determine a timeline for this important work to be undertaken.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure that freephone and other helpline numbers on the Universal Credit portal are (i) accessible; and (ii) fit for purpose.
answer_text: The Universal Credit Freephone helpline number 0800 012 1331 and textphone 0800 012 1441 is available within the Universal Credit portal and within nidirect. Details of the other helpline numbers such as Discretionary Support, Debt Management, Rate Rebate and Advice NI are also available through both channels. All information contained within the above platforms has been assessed to ensure its content, tone, language and structure are fit for purpose.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of recent reports from patients at Altnagelvin Area Hospital Emergency Department (ED), what action his Department is taking to address (i) the loss of clinical information between paramedics and ED staff; (ii) inadequate triage practices where patients are not directly assessed by a clinician; and (iii) delays of over 12 hours without treatment or pain relief.
answer_text: Loss of clinical information between paramedics and ED staffThe Western Trust have advised that they are not aware of any incidents and that a search of the Trust Datix system has not shown any incident where information was “lost” between NIAS personnel and/or ED staff. Inadequate triage practices where patients are not directly assessed by a clinicianThe Western Trust have senior staff nurses who are trained in the Manchester Triage System and triage all patients presenting to ED.   This a safe and tested method of the triage process carried out in all HSC hospitals in Northern Ireland.Delays of over 12 hours without treatment or pain reliefIt is the aim of the Trusts Emergency Department that patients are seen and assessed by a Clinician within four hours of arrival. Whilst the Trust strive to achieve these targets, this can be challenging for a number of reasons including overcrowding, increased attendances, specifically at pressure points during the day and clinical priority. However, when patients are seen at triage and when registered, they are offered analgesia for symptom management until further assessment can be provided. During times of significant delay in patient assessment, further assessment of patient need, such as further analgesia can be requested at any time. The Emergency Department have initiated, in July 2025, a Waiting Room Nurse who is tasked to support those patients in the waiting area who may require further assistance prior to clinical assessment by a doctor or clinician.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any action his Department is taking to support community-led regeneration schemes to encourage investment in rural towns.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to explain the delay in making appointments to the board of the Victims and Survivors Service.

To ask the Minister of Health to outline a timeframe for when he expects to receive the vision plan from the Western Health and Social Care Trust in relation to the future of emergency general surgery at the South West Acute Hospital.
answer_text: Following the pause in the consultation on Emergency General Surgery in the Western Trust, I have asked the Trust to use this immediate time to restore confidence by producing a vision plan emphasising how the South West Acute Hospital will be supported to meet both the needs of its current and future population.  I did not give the Trust a deadline for the completion of the plan as I would much rather this process be carried out correctly rather than be dictated by a timeframe. The Trust’s Board will meet on 2 October 20205, to make decisions on the current paused consultation, and the work ahead on a “Vision” for SWAH.  The Trust will work closely with staff, community leaders, elected representatives, the public and my officials as they prepare the Vision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the timescale for the delivery of Project Gigabit.
answer_text: Project Gigabit is still in the procurement phase as my officials, along with Building Digital UK (BDUK) are assessing new commercial rollout plans for the North that became available over the summer. That assessment is due to complete shortly, allowing the procurement to complete and a contract awarded.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the selection process for a driving assessment for those with disabilities.
answer_text: Where a driver makes a declaration that they have a medical condition, or concerns are raised from another source such as a medical practitioner, the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) will commence medical investigations to establish medical fitness to drive against the published standards.  More complex cases are referred to DVA’s contracted medical advisors for assessment. The medical advisors may be able to make an assessment based on the information supplied in the medical reports, may require further information from the driver’s medical practitioner or may refer the driver for specialist assessment such as a driving assessment to determine current driving needs and obtain advice regarding any adaptations which might assist driving.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what steps he has taken to ensure that public-facing advice and guidance on earwax removal services is improved.
answer_text: Following a number of questions recently on this subject, officials have contacted Trusts and Primary Care, highlighting the need to provide HSC public advice on ear wax removal.  In the first instance, however, patients should always contact their GP, Pharmacist or healthcare professional to seek advice on self-management of ear wax removal.  Patients can then be referred to either primary care or secondary care clinics to have ear wax removed.Unfortunately, as with many services in the HSC, demand in primary and secondary care exceeds capacity resulting in lengthening waiting times for treatment.  Recently a high street provider has offered free ear wax removal for over 60-year olds which is widely publicised in the media.My Department continues to optimise all available resources whilst seeking additional funding to improve waiting times.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what steps he has taken to ensure that those who need professional earwax removal services can access them.
answer_text: Ear wax removal services are available for patients locally in primary and/or secondary care across all Trusts with hospital services.   Some GPs provide ear wax removal services at their practice but in recent years, changes to National Institute of Clinical Excellence (NICE) clinical guidelines have resulted in fewer practices being able to treat patents.   Ear wax removal is now treated by using micro suction as a result of these guidelines.In the Northern Trust there is currently 1 full time ENT Nurse who undertakes this role, patients are prioritised according to those patients who meet the criteria and must have ongoing reviews within the service.The Southern Trust provides an ENT nurse led ear wax removal service in Craigavon Hospital on Tuesday afternoons, in Daisy Hill on Mondays and Banbridge Health and Care Centre on Monday and Wednesday afternoons. South Tyrone Hospital holds clinics twice a month.The South Eastern Trust provides ear wax removal at clinics in the Ulster Hospital, Lagan Valley Hospital & the Downe Hospital sites by trained ENT nurses.  Patients can be referred for micro-suction, however this service is only commissioned for patients where there is a clinical contraindication to the procedure in Primary care.  Those patients that are referred to the Trust are seen based on their clinical urgency.Hearing aid service users within Belfast HSC Trust with significant wax impacting hearing aid use or overall hearing are offered a referral to the nurse-led micro suction clinic in which they are given priority to access the system.In the Western Trust, full day clinics are held in Altnagelvin Hospital on Mondays, Tuesdays and Thursdays. A further clinic is held on Friday afternoons.Omagh Health Centre & Primary Care Complex holds full day clinics for ear care patients including post op mastoid surgery, otitis externa and complicated wax patients on Wednesdays.These clinics, however, are only seeing urgent/high risk and (GP) referral patients like Chronic otitis externa, tympanic membrane perforation, attic retraction, post op mastoidectomy patients, and otitis externa due to the backlog in outpatient appointments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his assessment of the process for calculating Child Maintenance, given that the income of resident parents is not taken into account for support payments.
answer_text: The existing child maintenance scheme is based on the principle a paying parent contributes a portion of their income to support their child, which represents their share of what an intact couple would spend on their children.The scheme does however reflect the individual circumstances of the paying parent, in that the calculation takes account of factors such as shared care of the qualifying children, other children living in the paying parent’s household, and other children the paying parent is paying maintenance for, as well as the paying parent’s income. For the purposes of child maintenance, the income of the parent with care is not taken into account. This is because the calculation represents an amount of money that is broadly commensurate with what a paying parent would spend on the child if they were still living with them, irrespective of the income or assets of the receiving parent.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what measures are to be taken to ensure access to mobile phone coverage for people in rural areas when O2 removes access to 2G and 3G services.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the impact of the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls funding at a local and regional level.
To ask the Assembly Commission to outline any plans to provide creche facilities for staff members in Parliament Buildings.
To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the baby loss certificate scheme.
answer_text: I am eager to move this scheme forward urgently to give all parents and families who have suffered the devastating loss of a baby formal recognition of their loss.I have met, and will continue to meet, with key stakeholders to take on board their views and input.I will be launching a public consultation tomorrow to ensure that everyone can provide their views to help us shape this scheme to meet the needs and expectations of those who have been impacted. The Deaths, Still-Births and Baby Loss Bill, which will provide a power to make regulations to form a scheme, remains at Committee Stage.  I remain hopeful that this stage will progress as quickly as possible.Once that process is complete my officials will draft the required secondary legislation and operationalise the scheme. If these matters progress smoothly, it remains my intention that the scheme be operational by the end of the year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the baby loss certificate scheme.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the implementation of the Graduated Driver Licensing scheme.
answer_text: I can advise that my officials have resumed work on the operational and legislative changes required to implement the graduated driver licensing scheme (GDL). This is a major project entailing significant amendment of current driver training, testing and post testing arrangements.  Once operational planning and the work my officials are doing on the legislative changes required have been sufficiently progressed, I will be able to provide an anticipated date for the introduction of GDL.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what discussions he has had with the Northern Ireland Medical and Dental Training Agency in terms of allocation and residency of doctors in (i) the Western Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) the South West Acute Hospital.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail where stem cell treatment for stroke patients is available.
answer_text: Stem cell treatment for stroke patients is not commissioned anywhere in Northern Ireland.Stroke services delivered in Northern Ireland are those with a robust evidence base and there is no evidence of benefit from the largest trials reported to date for stem cell treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the status of the business case for the redevelopment of Enniskillen Library.
answer_text: The addendum to the business case for the redevelopment of Enniskillen Library was approved in November 2024 subject to the availability of capital funding. I am pleased to confirm I have allocated capital funding of £756k in 2025/26 to progress the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are plans to carry out organ transplantations in Dublin.
answer_text: With the exception of kidney transplantation, all solid organ transplants are referred to highly specialised providers in the wider National Health Service (NHS) in Great Britain (GB).The volume of these organs available for transplant in the United Kingdom (UK) is such they can only be delivered by small number of highly specialised providers who have been accredited by National Health Service England Highly Specialised Services.The Northern Ireland arrangements for solid organ transplantation are part of a UK-wide agreement which allows access from the 4 nations within the UK to the total pool of organs donated by the total UK population.This also includes participation in the UK Living Kidney Sharing Scheme (UKLKSS) which further optimises access to the donated pool of kidneys.  The UKLKSS was established after the introduction of the Human Tissue Act 2004 and the Human Tissue (Scotland) Act (2006) and has since developed into one of the most successful schemes in Europe.When donor and recipient pairs are unable to donate due to being incompatible they are registered in the sharing scheme to find compatible matches with another donor and recipient pair from across the UK.  The Kidney Transplant Service in NI will do the associated work up and transplantation of their patients who are facilitated through the UKLKSS.Currently, there are no plans to change these arrangements to include organ transplantations in Dublin.  Consideration of using providers out-with the NHS is possible but would require a case to be made setting out why this was necessary given ready access to the pre-assured NHS network with established national oversight by the NHS Blood and Transplant (NHSBT) Team.  Achieving the same levels of assurance with providers in ROI is possible but given the differences in systems, these would need time to work through in detail to confirm that assurance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are plans to reinstate the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme.
answer_text: As outlined in my statement to the Assembly, I plan to establish a new Waiting List Reduction Reimbursement Scheme, as part of a package of initiatives to tackle hospital care backlogs.The Scheme will commence in June 2025 and will give patients, who are ordinarily resident in Northern Ireland, the option to seek and pay for treatment in the private sector and have the cost reimbursed, up to the cost of the treatment to HSC in Northern Ireland.  Patients will only be able to access the Scheme once they have waited on an HSC hospital treatment waiting list for two years.The new Scheme will be initiated on a phased approach.  The first phase will allow reimbursements for treatment in the Republic of Ireland (RoI). The second phase will be introduced at a later date and will see the geographical scope of the Scheme expanded to all countries in the European Union (EU).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the Chancellor's Spring Statement 2025.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the process for organ retrieval during a bank holiday.
answer_text: Organ retrieval is a nationally commissioned service delivered by NHS Blood and Transplant Authority. The service is provided 24/7 with no difference on weekend days or public holidays

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are any plans to introduce an extracorporeal membrane oxygenation machine in any Health Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO) is a life preserving technique of providing prolonged cardiac and respiratory support to persons whose heart and lungs are unable to provide adequate amount of gas exchange or perfusion to sustain life.The service is not available in Northern Ireland as there would not be an appropriate number of referrals each year to sustain local expertise.This service is provided in a small number of highly specialised centres elsewhere in the NHS. Arrangements are in place for people from NI who require ECMO to access this treatment in one of those centres and robust transport services are available to support patients in receiving this vital service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail why organ transplants, in particular lung transplants, cannot be carried out in the North.
answer_text: All solid organ transplants with the exception of kidney transplants, take place outside of Northern Ireland by highly specialised quaternary NHS provider Trusts in GB.In 2023/24, excluding kidney transplants, there were a total of 1,296 organ transplants undertaken for the UK population of 68.46m.  The volume of organ transplants is such that they can only safely be provided in a small number of highly specialist NHS designated provider Trusts which are configured to ensure clinical teams can maintain their skills at the required standard.With the exception of renal transplantation, the population of Northern Ireland is too small to support organ transplantation locally.Kidney transplantation including living donor transplantation is delivered by Belfast Trust and the vast majority of patients from Northern Ireland have their kidney transplants locally.The Department of Health oversees the commissioning of other organ transplantations to ensure patients from Northern Ireland have equitable access to the network of designated NHS Highly Specialised Providers.  Cardiac, thoracic, respiratory and intestinal clinicians in Northern Ireland have strong links with these providers and work closely to support patients both before and after their transplant.NHS Blood and Transplant monitor and routinely publish all aspects of organ retrieval and transplant for the UK and will identify and respond to any variances as indicated.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people on a waiting list for temporary or emergency accommodation in Fermanagh.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive does not operate waiting lists for temporary accommodation. Temporary accommodation is provided at the point of need to homeless applicants in line with our statutory duties under the Housing (Northern Ireland) Order 1988.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people currently recorded as homeless in Fermanagh.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“As of December 2024, there were 2,263 applicants on the Housing Executive’s waiting list in the Fermanagh & Omagh Local Government District. Of these, 1,160 were awarded Full Duty Applicant status.Full Duty Applicants are applicants accepted as being homeless, to whom the Housing Executive owes a duty under the Homelessness legislation, Article 10(2) of the Housing (Northern Ireland) Order 1988.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive estimate that the total gross cost of housing people in non-Housing Executive properties in 2023/24 equated to circa £36,261k. This gross figure includes a combination of both revenue (£34,190k) and capital (£2,071k) programme expenditure but does not take into account income received through Housing Benefit or Self Funder contributions.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NIHE directly. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people currently being housed in temporary or emergency accommodation in Fermanagh that is not owned by the Housing Executive.
answer_text: The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin April – September 2024, published on 12 December 2024, contains information on a range of areas relating to homelessness and is available at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-april-september-2024The accompanying tables at Section 3 contain details on temporary accommodation placements; however, I am unable to provide a breakdown in the format you have requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of Housing Executive temporary or emergency housing units in Fermanagh; and (ii) the number of people currently being housed in these units.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive provides temporary accommodation in line with its statutory as outlined in the Housing (NI) Order 1988.The availability of temporary accommodation is recorded by council area and as of 4 March 2025 there were a total of 224 units available within the Fermanagh and Omagh District Council area.
